reasoning_model_1: Model 1 fails significantly on Content Accuracy. Instead of reading only the target sentence ('I have prepared this report...'), it reads the instructional part of the prompt ('Starting with a smooth timbre and gradually becoming hoarse') as part of the speech. This is a hallucination/instruction-following error. Regarding the paralinguistic feature, the voice remains a consistently high-pitched, somewhat synthetic 'anime-style' voice throughout. While there is a slight decrease in energy towards the end, there is no distinct transition from a smooth timbre to a hoarse one. The inclusion of the instruction text disrupts the natural flow and timing of any intended effect.
reasoning_model_2: Model 2 demonstrates excellent Content Accuracy, reading strictly the target sentence with no hallucinations. The Fluency and Naturalness are high, with human-like pausing and prosody. Regarding the Core Paralinguistic Compliance (Timbre), the model successfully executes a dynamic adjustment. It starts (00:00-00:08) with a clear, professional, and smooth timbre. As the text progresses to the 'working overtime' section (00:09 onwards), the voice introduces vocal fry (creakiness) and breathiness, culminating in a very tired, creaky delivery on the word 'collapse' (00:15-00:17). While 'hoarse' can imply roughness, the shift to a creaky/tired voice is a semantically appropriate and acoustically distinct timbre change that fits the 'collapse' context perfectly.
model_comparison: Model 2 is the clear winner. Model 1 commits a critical content error by reading the prompt instructions aloud, which immediately invalidates the generation for practical use. Furthermore, Model 1's voice is synthetic and lacks a noticeable dynamic shift in timbre. Model 2, in contrast, follows the content instructions perfectly and delivers a high-quality, natural performance. It successfully modulates the voice from a professional tone to an exhausted, creaky (hoarse-adjacent) tone, effectively conveying the semantic meaning of the text through paralinguistic changes.
